$comment: Rich, kid-facing content for featured elements. 'facts' must be scientifically true; 'imagine' is clearly-labeled playful metaphor and must never contradict the facts. props: hardness=Mohs scale, density=g/cm3 (gases at room conditions), meltC=melting point Celsius, conduct=qualitative electrical conductivity 0-10 for kid-facing bars only. Values verified vs CRC/IUPAC 2026-07-06; per-value citations and uncertainty flags in docs/SOURCES.md.

facts: Hydrogen is the lightest element and the most common one in the whole universe.
Stars like our Sun shine by squeezing hydrogen atoms together — that's called fusion.
Two hydrogen atoms plus one oxygen atom make a water molecule: H₂O.
1.imagine: If elements were balloons, hydrogen would be the one that escapes your hand first and floats highest.
1.origin: Almost all hydrogen was made in the Big Bang, nearly 14 billion years ago. It's the original element!
trivia: Rockets can burn hydrogen as fuel — the exhaust is mostly water vapor.
About 9 out of every 10 atoms in the universe are hydrogen.

facts: Helium is lighter than air, so helium balloons float up.
It's a noble gas: it almost never reacts or joins with other elements.
Liquid helium is the coldest liquid there is — close to −269 °C. It cools the giant magnets inside MRI machines at hospitals.
2.imagine: Helium is the calm loner of the periodic table — it floats above everything and doesn't team up with anyone.
2.origin: Helium was discovered in the Sun before it was found on Earth — scientists spotted it in sunlight in 1868. Its name comes from 'helios', Greek for Sun.
trivia: Airships and blimps float using helium because it can't catch fire.
Earth's helium slowly escapes to space — once a balloon's helium gets out, it's gone for good.

facts: Lithium is the lightest metal of all — a chunk of it would float on water (but it reacts with water, so that's a lab-only fact!).
The rechargeable batteries in phones, laptops, and electric cars are lithium batteries.
Lithium is one of only three elements made in the Big Bang, along with hydrogen and helium.
3.imagine: Lithium is a featherweight boxer with a mighty punch — the lightest metal there is, yet it powers your whole tablet.
3.origin: Discovered in 1817 inside a stone — its name comes from 'lithos', Greek for stone. It's as old as the universe itself: some lithium atoms were made in the Big Bang.
trivia: An electric car battery can hold several kilograms of lithium.
Lithium burns with a crimson-red flame — that red in some fireworks? Often lithium.

facts: The James Webb Space Telescope's giant golden mirrors are made of beryllium — super light, super stiff, and steady in extreme cold.
Emeralds and aquamarines are beryllium minerals (the mineral beryl) with a dash of other elements for color.
Beryllium dust is harmful to breathe, so factories handle it with serious protection.
4.imagine: Beryllium is the featherweight champion with nerves of steel — so calm and stiff that we trust it to hold a telescope's gaze across the universe.
4.origin: Discovered in 1798 inside beryl gemstones. Early chemists called it 'glucinium' because its compounds taste sweet — which they definitely should NOT have tested by licking!
trivia: Beryllium is see-through to X-rays, so X-ray machine 'windows' are made from it.
It's about a third lighter than aluminum but much stiffer.

facts: Boron makes glass heat-proof: lab beakers and oven dishes are borosilicate glass, which barely expands when hot, so it doesn't crack.
Boron compounds make slime slimy — borax links the glue molecules together into a stretchy net.
Plants can't grow without a tiny bit of boron in the soil.
5.imagine: Boron is the quiet kid who makes the whole team better — add a pinch to glass, metal, or slime and suddenly it's tougher, springier, stretchier.
5.origin: Borax has been used for over a thousand years (ancient goldsmiths used it for soldering), but pure boron was only isolated in 1808 — and truly pure boron not until a century later.
trivia: Boron burns with a bright green flame — some green fireworks use it.
Boron carbide is one of the hardest materials ever made — it's used in tank armor.

facts: Carbon atoms can arrange themselves as diamond (the hardest natural material) or as graphite (so soft it rubs off as pencil marks). Same atoms, different arrangement!
Every living thing — you, trees, dogs, mushrooms — is built around carbon.
How atoms are arranged changes what a material can do. That's a big idea in material science.
6.imagine: Carbon is like building blocks: stack them one way and you get an unbreakable castle (diamond); stack them in flat sheets and they slide apart (graphite).
6.origin: Carbon is made inside stars. The carbon in your body was forged in a star that existed before the Sun.
trivia: A pencil 'lead' has no lead in it — it's graphite, a form of carbon.
Diamond is a 10 out of 10 on the hardness scale — nothing in nature scratches it.

facts: About 78% of the air you're breathing right now is nitrogen.
Plants need nitrogen to grow — it's a main ingredient in fertilizer.
Liquid nitrogen is extremely cold: about −196 °C.
7.imagine: Nitrogen is the quiet crowd filling the stadium of the sky — it's most of the air, but you never notice it.
7.origin: Made inside stars, like carbon and oxygen. Earth's thick nitrogen atmosphere has been here for billions of years.
trivia: Scientists use liquid nitrogen to freeze things in seconds.
Some race cars fill tires with nitrogen instead of regular air.

facts: You breathe oxygen to stay alive — about 21% of air is oxygen.
Fire needs oxygen to burn. No oxygen, no flame.
Oxygen is the most common element in Earth's rocky crust, locked inside rocks, sand, and water.
8.imagine: Oxygen is the friendliest element at the party — it grabs hands with almost everyone (that's why so many materials contain it).
8.origin: Made inside big stars. Earth's breathable oxygen came from tiny ocean life and plants making it for billions of years.
trivia: Rust is what happens when oxygen slowly 'grabs' iron.
Water is one oxygen atom holding two hydrogens.

facts: Fluorine is the most reactive element of all — it grabs onto almost anything it touches. Pure fluorine gas is dangerous, so only scientists ever handle it.
Tamed into 'fluoride' and added to toothpaste in tiny amounts, it makes your tooth enamel harder.
Teflon — the slippery non-stick coating on pans — is made of carbon and fluorine.
9.imagine: Fluorine is the wildest kid in the element class who grew up to be a bodyguard — locked safely inside toothpaste, it protects your teeth every single morning.
9.origin: Fluorine was so fierce that chemists were injured trying to capture it for decades. Henri Moissan finally tamed it in 1886 — and won a Nobel Prize for it. Its name comes from 'fluere', Latin for 'to flow'.
trivia: Fluorine helps make one of the slipperiest solids ever created (the Teflon on non-stick pans).
Brushing with fluoride toothpaste turns the outside of your teeth into a tougher mineral.

facts: When electricity zaps through neon gas, it glows a bright red-orange.
Neon is a noble gas — it doesn't react with other elements.
Neon is rare on Earth; it's collected from air a tiny bit at a time.
10.imagine: Neon is a shy element that only shows off when you plug it in — then it becomes the brightest sign on the street.
10.origin: Discovered in 1898 by chilling air until it turned to liquid and separating out the gases. Its name means 'new' in Greek.
trivia: Only the red-orange glowing signs are true neon — other colors use different gases.
There's more neon in stars than on Earth.

facts: Sodium is a metal so soft you could cut it with a butter knife.
Pure sodium reacts violently with water — scientists keep it under oil. (Never touch pure sodium — this is a lab-only fact!)
Team it up with chlorine and it calms down completely: together they make table salt, which is safe to eat.
11.imagine: Sodium alone is a wild firecracker; with its buddy chlorine it becomes the salt on your fries. Teamwork changes everything.
11.origin: Its symbol Na comes from 'natrium', an old Latin word. It was first separated from salt compounds in 1807 using electricity.
trivia: Salt crystals are tiny cubes — sodium and chlorine atoms stack in a perfect cube pattern.
Some streetlights glow yellow-orange because of sodium inside.

facts: Magnesium burns with a light so bright and white that it was used for camera flashes long ago — and still lights up fireworks and emergency flares today.
Every green plant needs magnesium: it sits at the center of chlorophyll, the molecule that catches sunlight.
It's a very light metal, so it's mixed into alloys for laptops, bikes, and race-car wheels.
12.imagine: Magnesium is the element world's camera flash — quiet and gray until showtime, then FLASH: brighter than anything else on stage.
12.origin: Named after Magnesia, a region in Greece where its minerals were dug up. First separated as a pure metal in 1808 by Humphry Davy.
trivia: The ocean is full of magnesium — seawater is one of the main places we get it from.
Your muscles need magnesium to work properly. It's in nuts, beans, and leafy greens.

13.superpower: Light as a feather, tough as a plane
facts: Aluminum is a strong metal that weighs about a third as much as iron.
It doesn't rust away like iron — it instantly grows an invisible protective skin of aluminum oxide.
It's the most common metal in Earth's crust.
13.imagine: Aluminum is the gymnast of metals — light, quick, and surprisingly strong.
13.origin: Long ago aluminum was harder to get than gold — Emperor Napoleon III served special guests with aluminum forks! In 1886 a cheap way to make it with electricity was invented, and now it's everywhere.
trivia: Recycling one aluminum can saves enough energy to run a TV for hours.
Aluminum can be recycled over and over without wearing out.

facts: Beach sand is mostly silicon and oxygen (silicon dioxide, also called quartz).
Silicon is a 'semiconductor': it can switch electricity on and off, which is how computer chips think.
It's the second most common element in Earth's crust, after oxygen.
14.imagine: Silicon went from lazing on the beach to running every computer, phone, and game console — the greatest career change of any element.
14.origin: Made in stars. Humans learned to purify it into shiny gray crystals for electronics in the 1900s.
trivia: 'Silicon Valley' is named after this element because computer chips are made from it.
Glass is made by melting sand — so windows are mostly silicon and oxygen too.

facts: The striking strip on a matchbox contains red phosphorus — it helps the match burst into flame.
Your bones and teeth are built from calcium phosphate, and the DNA inside every one of your cells has a phosphorus backbone.
There's another form, white phosphorus, that glows in the dark and catches fire in air by itself — scientists keep it underwater, and nobody should ever touch it.
15.imagine: Phosphorus is the battery pack of every living cell — it stores little packets of energy the way a wallet stores pocket money.
15.origin: Discovered in 1669 when Hennig Brand boiled down urine hunting for gold — and found a glowing element instead! It was the first element discovery in history where we know exactly who found it. The name means 'light-bearer' in Greek.
trivia: The 'P' in NPK plant fertilizer stands for phosphorus — crops can't grow without it.
Phosphorus never appears pure in nature — it's far too eager to react.

facts: Sulfur is a yellow solid found near volcanoes.
Many smelly things — rotten eggs, skunk spray — get their stink from sulfur compounds.
Adding sulfur to rubber makes it strong and bouncy — that's how car tires are made (it's called vulcanization).
16.imagine: Sulfur is the smelly superhero: nobody wants to sit next to it, but your bike tires wouldn't survive without it.
16.origin: Known since ancient times — it's one of the few elements found pure in nature, often near volcanoes.
trivia: Pure sulfur is bright lemon-yellow.
Match heads contain sulfur to help them ignite.

facts: Chlorine keeps swimming pools and drinking water safe by destroying germs.
Pure chlorine is a green-yellow gas that is dangerous to breathe — that's why only trained adults handle pool chemicals.
With sodium it makes table salt — completely safe to eat.
17.imagine: Chlorine is a fierce guard dog: scary on its own, but leashed up with sodium it's as gentle as the salt on your popcorn.
17.origin: Discovered in 1774. Its name comes from 'chloros', Greek for green-yellow, because of the gas's color.
trivia: That 'pool smell' is chlorine doing its germ-fighting job.
The ocean is salty largely thanks to chloride.

facts: About 1 in every 100 breaths you take is argon — it's the third most common gas in the air.
Its name means 'the lazy one' in Greek, because it almost never reacts with anything.
That laziness is its superpower: argon fills light bulbs and blankets hot metal during welding, because it refuses to let things burn.
18.imagine: Argon is the bodyguard who wins every fight by doing absolutely nothing — nothing can make it react.
18.origin: Discovered in 1894, hiding in plain sight: scientists noticed the 'nitrogen' from air weighed a tiny bit too much, and found a whole new gas inside. It was the first noble gas ever found on Earth.
trivia: Double-glazed windows often have argon sealed between the panes to keep heat in.
Argon protects old documents and museum treasures from slowly reacting with air.

facts: Every blink, heartbeat, and wiggle of your toes is a signal sent by your nerves using potassium (teamed up with sodium).
Bananas, potatoes, and orange juice are famous for being full of potassium.
Pure potassium metal reacts with water even more fiercely than sodium — with a lilac flash! Strictly a lab-only fact.
19.imagine: Potassium is your body's text-messaging service — millions of tiny potassium-powered signals zip through your nerves every second.
19.origin: In 1807, Humphry Davy used electricity to split potassium out of 'potash' — ashes from burnt plants soaked in a pot. That's really where the name comes from! Its symbol K is from 'kalium', its Latin name.
trivia: Potassium was the very first metal ever separated using electricity.
Potassium burns with a pretty lilac-purple flame.

facts: Your bones and teeth get their strength from calcium.
Chalk, limestone cliffs, and seashells are all made of calcium carbonate.
Milk, cheese, and yogurt are famous for having lots of calcium.
20.imagine: Calcium is the construction crew inside your body, building your skeleton a little stronger every day.
20.origin: Its name comes from 'calx', Latin for lime (the rock, not the fruit). It was first separated as a pure metal in 1808.
trivia: The white cliffs of Dover in England are giant walls of calcium carbonate made from ancient sea creatures.
Eggshells are mostly calcium carbonate too.

facts: A pinch of scandium makes aluminum alloys noticeably stronger — used in fighter jets, racing bikes, and baseball bats.
Dmitri Mendeleev predicted scandium before anyone found it — he even predicted its properties, and he was right.
It's named after Scandinavia, where it was discovered.
21.imagine: Scandium is the secret ingredient — a spoonful in a swimming pool of aluminum, and the whole batch levels up.
21.origin: Discovered in 1879 in Sweden by Lars Fredrik Nilson — filling the exact gap Mendeleev had labeled 'eka-boron' ten years earlier.
trivia: Some stadium lights use scandium to shine more like daylight.
Scandium is scattered so thinly around Earth that it's expensive even though it isn't truly rare.

facts: Titanium is as strong as many steels but almost half the weight.
It doesn't rust, even in seawater.
Doctors use it for replacement hips and dental implants because the body accepts it well.
22.imagine: Titanium is the knight in featherweight armor — tough enough for battle, light enough to run.
22.origin: Named after the Titans, the giant gods of Greek myths, because it's so strong. Discovered in 1791.
trivia: Fighter jets and spacecraft use titanium parts.
Some bike frames and glasses frames are titanium — strong, light, and they last forever.

facts: Vanadium steel is famously tough — it helped make early cars strong and light, and it's still in wrenches and axles today.
In chemistry class demos, vanadium solutions can shift through four different colors: yellow, blue, green, and violet.
It's named after Vanadis, a name of the Norse goddess of beauty, because its compounds are so colorful.
23.imagine: Vanadium is a knight who forges unbreakable armor by day and paints rainbows by night.
23.origin: Discovered twice! First in Mexico in 1801 (but talked out of it), then again in Sweden in 1830, where it got its goddess name.
trivia: Some sea squirts mysteriously collect vanadium in their blood — scientists still puzzle over why.
Giant vanadium 'flow batteries' can store energy for whole towns.

facts: Chromium's name comes from the Greek word for color — rubies are red and emeralds are green because of tiny amounts of chromium inside them.
'Chrome' — the mirror-bright coating on faucets, tools, and old car bumpers — is chromium.
Chromium teams up with iron and nickel to make stainless steel stainless.
24.imagine: Chromium is the artist AND the polisher: it paints gemstones from the inside, then shines everything else like a mirror.
24.origin: Discovered in 1797 in a bright red Siberian mineral. Chemists soon realized this one element could make red, green, yellow, and orange colors.
trivia: School bus yellow was originally a chromium paint color.
A ruby and an emerald owe their colors to the same element in different homes.

facts: Cave painters used black manganese minerals more than 17,000 years ago — some of humanity's first art used this element.
A little manganese makes steel tough enough for railroad tracks, safes, and bulldozer blades.
Your bones and the enzymes in your body need a trace of manganese.
25.imagine: Manganese is the ancient art teacher who now coaches a football team of steel girders.
25.origin: Recognized as an element in 1774 in Sweden. Its confusing name comes from the same magnetic minerals region as magnesium — the two were mixed up for ages.
trivia: Parts of the deep ocean floor are scattered with potato-sized manganese nodules that grew over millions of years.
Most drink cans contain a little manganese to make the aluminum stronger.

facts: Iron is the most-used metal on Earth. Mixed with a little carbon it becomes steel.
Earth's core is mostly iron — it's what gives our planet its magnetic field.
Your blood is red because of iron inside a molecule called hemoglobin, which carries oxygen.
26.imagine: Iron is the strong, dependable builder of the element world — skyscrapers, bridges, and even your blood count on it.
26.origin: Before people learned to melt iron from rocks, the first iron tools were made from meteorites — metal that literally fell from the sky!
trivia: Magnets stick to iron — try it on a steel fridge.
Rust is iron slowly combining with oxygen and water.

facts: Cobalt blue has colored glass and pottery for centuries — ancient Egyptian and Chinese artists prized it.
Cobalt sits at the heart of vitamin B12, which your body needs to make blood cells.
It's one of only three elements that are magnetic at room temperature (with iron and nickel), and it's key to many rechargeable batteries.
27.imagine: Cobalt is a goblin from old mining legends who turned out to be an artist — grumpy name, gorgeous blue.
27.origin: Named after the 'kobolds' — goblins that German miners blamed for ore that looked valuable but wasn't. In 1735, chemist Georg Brandt proved the 'goblin ore' held a brand-new metal.
trivia: Some 'invisible inks' use cobalt: the writing appears when the paper is gently warmed.
Cobalt keeps jet-engine parts strong at scorching temperatures.

facts: Mix nickel with titanium and you get 'nitinol', a memory metal: bend it out of shape, warm it up, and it springs back to exactly how it was.
Nickel makes steel stainless — it's a key ingredient in rust-proof forks, sinks, and pans.
Deep down, Earth's core is a giant ball of iron and nickel.
28.imagine: Nickel is the loyal sidekick of the metal world — rarely the star, but mixed into other metals it makes them tougher, shinier, and rust-proof.
28.origin: German miners kept finding ore that looked like copper but wasn't — they blamed a mischievous goblin and called it 'Kupfernickel', devil's copper. In 1751 a Swedish chemist showed it was a brand-new metal.
trivia: The US 5-cent 'nickel' coin is actually mostly copper — just one quarter of it is nickel.
Magnets stick to nickel! Only three elements are magnetic at room temperature: iron, cobalt, and nickel.
Real meteorites almost always contain nickel mixed with iron — it's one way scientists spot a fake.

facts: Copper carries electricity extremely well — the wires in your walls are copper.
Copper starts shiny orange-brown and slowly turns green outdoors. The Statue of Liberty is copper — that's why she's green!
Germs have a hard time surviving on copper surfaces.
29.imagine: Copper is the delivery driver of the element world, rushing electric charge from the power plant to your game console.
29.origin: One of the first metals humans ever used — copper tools are over 9,000 years old. Mixed with tin it made bronze and kicked off the Bronze Age.
trivia: Old pennies look brown because of copper on the outside.
Copper + zinc = brass, the metal of trumpets and door handles.

facts: Coating steel with zinc protects it from rust — it's called galvanizing.
Zinc mixed with copper makes brass.
Your body needs a tiny bit of zinc to heal cuts and fight germs.
30.imagine: Zinc is the bodyguard that jumps in front of iron and takes the rust hit itself.
30.origin: Used for thousands of years in brass, but only recognized as its own element in Europe in the 1700s.
trivia: Many coins are mostly zinc inside with a thin coating outside.
Sunscreen sometimes contains zinc oxide — the white kind lifeguards wear on their noses.

facts: Gallium melts at just under 30 °C — a piece would slowly melt into a silvery puddle in your warm hand (a famous science-fair demonstration).
Gallium nitride makes blue LEDs possible — the invention that completed white LED light and won a Nobel Prize in 2014.
Gallium never appears as a pure metal in nature; we get it as a bonus while refining aluminum.
31.imagine: Gallium is the prankster metal: it looks solid and serious like aluminum, then melts the moment things warm up.
31.origin: Another Mendeleev prediction come true! He described 'eka-aluminum' in 1871; French chemist Lecoq de Boisbaudran found real gallium in 1875, matching almost perfectly.
trivia: A classic (mean!) chemistry prank: a gallium spoon that melts in hot tea.
Gallium stays liquid over a huge range — from ~30 °C to over 2,000 °C.

facts: The very first transistor — ancestor of every computer chip — was made of germanium in 1947, before silicon took over.
Germanium is transparent to infrared light, so night-vision and heat-camera lenses are made from it.
A dash of germanium in the core of fiber-optic cables helps guide light around the world.
32.imagine: Germanium is the retired champion who taught silicon everything it knows.
32.origin: Mendeleev's most famous prediction: he described 'eka-silicon' down to its density. When Clemens Winkler found it in 1886, the match made the periodic table world-famous.
trivia: Night-vision goggles see the world through germanium glass.
Winkler named it after his homeland, Germany.

facts: Arsenic is famously poisonous — it's the villain of countless old detective stories, and nobody should ever touch it.
In safe, sealed form, gallium arsenide chips help phones and satellites send signals — some jobs silicon can't do.
Scientists once found arsenic in wallpaper pigments and old medicines — banning it made homes much safer.
33.imagine: Arsenic is the classic story villain who, under strict supervision, now works an honest job in electronics.
33.origin: Known since ancient times; credit for isolating it often goes to the medieval scholar Albertus Magnus around the year 1250 — making it one of the oldest known elements.
trivia: A once-popular green paint ('Paris green') was dropped when people learned it contained arsenic.
Forensic scientists learned to detect arsenic in the 1830s — and the 'undetectable poison' era ended.

facts: Selenium conducts electricity better when light shines on it — early photocopiers, light meters, and solar cells were built on this trick.
Your body needs a trace of selenium; Brazil nuts are famously packed with it.
It's named after Selene, the Greek Moon goddess — its neighbor tellurium was already named after the Earth.
34.imagine: Selenium is a sunflower in metal form — it wakes up and springs into action whenever light hits it.
34.origin: Discovered in 1817 by Jöns Jacob Berzelius, who noticed a strange red residue in his sulfuric acid factory.
trivia: The first television experiments in the 1800s used selenium to turn light into electric signals.
Red glass and some red paints get their color from selenium.

facts: Bromine is one of only two elements that are liquid at room temperature — the other is mercury.
It's a deep red-brown liquid that gives off orange fumes, and it's harsh stuff: only trained chemists handle it.
Its name comes from the Greek word for 'stench' — it smells terrible!
35.imagine: Bromine is the dragon of the element world: red-brown, smoky, and definitely not for petting.
35.origin: Discovered in 1826 by Antoine Balard, a 23-year-old French scientist studying salty pond water from the sea.
trivia: Old-fashioned photography worked thanks to silver bromide — light falling on it drew the picture.
The Dead Sea is one of the richest bromine sources on Earth.

facts: Krypton's name means 'the hidden one' in Greek — it took clever chemistry with liquid air to find it.
From 1960 to 1983, the world's official METER was defined using the orange light of krypton atoms.
Like all noble gases, it almost never reacts with anything.
36.imagine: Krypton is a shy celebrity: it hid from scientists for centuries, then quietly helped define how long a meter is.
36.origin: Discovered in 1898 by William Ramsay and Morris Travers, who boiled liquid air and studied what was left behind.
trivia: Sorry, Superman fans — kryptonite is fiction, but krypton is a real (and completely harmless) gas.
Krypton can make brilliant white light in some camera flashes and airport runway lights.

facts: Rubidium atomic clocks ride on GPS satellites — your map app works because rubidium atoms tick with incredible steadiness.
It's even more reactive than potassium: pure rubidium must be sealed away from air and water.
It was discovered through a spectroscope — spotted by the deep red lines in its light, which gave it its name (rubidus = deep red).
37.imagine: Rubidium is the world's most reliable drummer — it never, ever misses a beat, so we let it keep time for the whole planet.
37.origin: Discovered in 1861 by Bunsen and Kirchhoff (of Bunsen-burner fame) using their brand-new invention: reading elements from the colors of their light.
trivia: Rubidium burns with a red-violet flame.
It's soft enough to cut with a butter knife — but you'd better not, because it ignites in air.

facts: The brilliant crimson-red bursts in fireworks are strontium salts burning.
Glow-in-the-dark toys and stickers often use strontium aluminate — it soaks up light, then glows for hours.
Your bones treat strontium like calcium's cousin and happily store tiny amounts of it.
38.imagine: Strontium is the firework artist who paints the sky red, then heads home to charge up your glow stars.
38.origin: Named after Strontian, a small village in Scotland where its mineral was first found in 1790.
trivia: It's one of the few elements named after a place in Scotland.
Old TV screens contained strontium to block stray X-rays.

facts: Silver conducts electricity better than any other element — even better than copper.
It's too expensive for everyday wires, so we save it for special jobs.
Mirrors get their perfect shine from a super-thin layer of silver or aluminum.
47.imagine: Silver is the Olympic gold medalist of conducting electricity — copper does the daily work, but silver holds the record.
47.origin: Treasured for thousands of years. Its symbol Ag comes from 'argentum', Latin for silver — the country Argentina is named after it.
trivia: Silver slowly darkens ('tarnishes') as it reacts with tiny traces of sulfur in the air.
Some bandages contain silver because it slows germ growth.

facts: Around 5,000 years ago, people mixed tin with copper to make bronze — a metal much harder than either one alone. A mixture of metals is called an alloy.
"Tin cans" are actually steel cans wearing a thin tin jacket to stop rust.
When you bend a bar of pure tin, it makes a crackling sound called the 'tin cry'.
50.imagine: Tin is the ultimate teammate — average alone, but team it with copper and together they started the Bronze Age.
50.origin: One of the oldest known metals. Bronze tools and weapons were so important that a whole age of history is named after the alloy.
trivia: The 'tin cry' crackle is the sound of crystals inside the metal twinning as they bend.
Solder, the metal glue of electronics, often contains tin.

facts: Heat solid iodine and it skips being a liquid — it turns straight into a beautiful violet vapor. Its name comes from the Greek for 'violet'.
The thyroid gland in your neck needs iodine to work — that's why table salt is often 'iodized'.
Iodine is a famous germ fighter: that brown liquid painted on cuts and scrapes.
53.imagine: Iodine is a tiny purple genie — warm up the dark crystals and a violet cloud whooshes out.
53.origin: Discovered by accident in 1811, when French chemist Bernard Courtois was processing seaweed ash and a mysterious purple vapor rose up.
trivia: Seaweed is naturally rich in iodine — one reason it's a healthy snack.
Scientists test for starch using iodine: it turns potato or bread spots blue-black.

facts: Real spacecraft fly using xenon: ion engines shoot xenon atoms out the back to push through space, gently but for years.
Xenon makes the brilliant white light in some car headlights and giant movie projectors.
Its name means 'stranger' in Greek — it's one of the rarest gases in our air.
54.imagine: Xenon is the quiet stranger in town who turns out to be a starship pilot.
54.origin: Discovered in 1898 by Ramsay and Travers — the third noble gas they found in liquid air in a single amazing summer.
trivia: NASA's Dawn spacecraft rode xenon ion engines all the way to the asteroid belt.
Doctors have used xenon gas to put patients to sleep for surgery.

facts: One SECOND is officially defined by cesium: the time it takes a cesium atom to vibrate exactly 9,192,631,770 times.
It's one of the very few metals with a golden color, and it melts on a hot day (28 °C).
It's the most reactive metal you could ever actually keep in a lab (its cousin francium is too rare to bottle).
55.imagine: Cesium is the golden metronome of the universe — every clock, phone, and satellite on Earth ultimately checks its beat.
55.origin: Discovered in 1860 by Bunsen and Kirchhoff — the first element ever found with a spectroscope. Named from 'caesius', Latin for sky-blue, after its bright blue spectral lines.
trivia: Cesium atomic clocks won't drift by even one second in millions of years.
Drop cesium in water and it explodes instantly — which is why nobody ever does (except in sealed lab demos!).

facts: Doctors give patients a perfectly safe 'barium meal' — a thick barium drink that makes the stomach and intestines show up clearly on X-rays.
Barium's name comes from the Greek 'barys', meaning heavy.
Green fireworks get their color from barium salts.
56.imagine: Barium is the stage crew that paints your insides with highlighter so the X-ray camera can see the show.
56.origin: Isolated in 1808 by Humphry Davy — the same electricity wizard who first separated sodium, potassium, calcium, and magnesium.
trivia: The safe barium drink is barium sulfate — it passes straight through without dissolving.
A barium compound was part of the first known glow-in-the-dark material, the 'Bologna stone' of the 1600s.

facts: Tungsten has the highest melting point of all metals: 3,422 °C.
That's why old-style light bulbs used a tungsten wire — it glows white-hot without melting.
Tungsten is also incredibly dense — a small block feels shockingly heavy.
74.imagine: If elements had a 'survive a volcano' contest, tungsten would win without breaking a sweat.
74.origin: Its name means 'heavy stone' in Swedish. Its symbol W comes from its other name, wolfram.
trivia: Tungsten is about as dense as gold.
Drill tips and rocket parts use tungsten because it laughs at heat.

facts: Gold never rusts or tarnishes — ancient gold treasures are still shiny today.
It's so soft and stretchy you can hammer it into sheets thinner than paper (gold leaf).
Scientists think Earth's gold was made in gigantic space events like colliding neutron stars.
79.imagine: Gold is the element that never ages — a pirate's coin from 300 years ago still gleams like new.
79.origin: Every atom of gold on Earth is older than the planet itself, forged in dying stars and star collisions, then gathered into the forming Earth.
trivia: Your phone contains a tiny amount of gold in its circuits, because gold never corrodes.
One gram of gold can be stretched into a wire more than a kilometer long.

facts: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature.
It's poisonous, so modern thermometers use safe liquids or electronics instead.
It's so dense that a chunk of iron would float on it like a boat.
80.imagine: Mercury is a metal that decided to be a puddle — shiny, silvery, and always on the move. Look, but never touch!
80.origin: Known to ancient civilizations, who found it dripping from a red rock called cinnabar. Its symbol Hg comes from 'hydrargyrum' — Greek for 'water-silver'.
trivia: Mercury the element is named after the speedy Roman messenger god, just like the planet.
Old-time hat makers were poisoned by mercury fumes — that's where 'mad as a hatter' may come from.

facts: Lead is very dense and soft — a lead brick is astonishingly heavy for its size.
It blocks X-rays, which is why the dentist's protective apron has lead inside.
Lead is toxic if it gets into the body, so it's been removed from paint, gasoline, and water pipes.
82.imagine: Lead is the heavyweight bouncer of the elements — even X-rays can't get past it.
82.origin: The ancient Romans made water pipes from lead. Their word for it, 'plumbum', gives lead its symbol Pb — and gives us the word 'plumber'!
trivia: Pencil 'lead' was never lead — it's graphite (carbon). People long ago mixed the two up.
Car batteries still use lead safely sealed inside.

facts: Astatine is the rarest natural element: all of it in Earth's crust at any moment adds up to less than a spoonful.
Its atoms fall apart within hours — its name literally means 'unstable' in Greek.
Nobody has ever seen a visible piece of astatine. If you somehow gathered one, its own radiation energy would instantly vaporize it.
85.imagine: Astatine is a magician frozen mid-disappearing-act — by the time you point at it, it's already gone.
85.origin: First created in 1940 at a particle accelerator in Berkeley, California — humans made it before anyone found it in nature.
trivia: Scientists study astatine one atom at a time.
Doctors are researching a form of astatine that might one day zap cancer cells with pinpoint accuracy.

facts: Radon is an invisible radioactive gas that seeps out of ordinary rocks and soil.
It can slowly collect in basements — which is why grown-ups can buy simple radon test kits, and some houses have special fans to blow it out.
Radon is born when radium deep in the ground breaks apart, and radon's own atoms break apart in a few days.
86.imagine: Radon is a real ghost that drifts up from underground — but unlike storybook ghosts, a simple detector finds it and a fan chases it away.
86.origin: Discovered in 1900 by Friedrich Dorn, who noticed radium giving off a mysterious radioactive gas — it was called 'radium emanation' at first.
trivia: Radon is the heaviest of all the noble gases.
Testing a home for radon is one of the easiest pieces of real science a family can do.

facts: Francium is the most unstable element found in nature — half of any francium disappears every 22 minutes.
At any moment, Earth's entire crust holds only a small pinch of it.
It was discovered in 1939 by Marguerite Perey, a scientist who started as Marie Curie's lab assistant at age 19 — she named it after France.
87.imagine: Francium is a snowman in the summer sun — real, but melting away almost as fast as it appears.
87.origin: The last element ever discovered in nature (rather than made by machines). Marguerite Perey found it hiding in the decay of actinium.
trivia: Scientists have never gathered enough francium to see it with the naked eye.
Perey became the first woman elected to the French Academy of Sciences.

facts: Radium was discovered in 1898 by Marie and Pierre Curie, who worked through tonnes of rock to collect a tiny speck of it.
It's about a million times more radioactive than uranium, and it glows a ghostly pale blue-green.
A century ago it was painted on watch dials before people understood radiation — a sad lesson that made science much safer today.
88.imagine: Radium is the too-bright firefly of the elements: dazzling, famous, and handled today only by scientists in serious protective gear.
88.origin: The Curies' discovery of radium (and polonium) won Marie Curie her place in history — she remains the only person with Nobel Prizes in two different sciences.
trivia: Marie Curie's own notebooks are still radioactive and are kept in lead-lined boxes.
Radium's glow gave us the word 'radioactivity' itself — coined by Marie Curie.
